text: The video shows a total laparoscopic hysterectomy. An ovarian cyst is visible, and the left ureter can be seen below the posterior leaf of the broad ligament. The uterosacral ligament is identified, and a 5mm Ligasure sealing device is used to begin the procedure.

The surgeon cuts the left round ligament and opens the peritoneum between the left round ligament and the infundibulopelvic (IP) ligament. The peritoneum is retracted to reveal the dissecting plane, allowing for wide opening of the retroperitoneal area. The section continues laterally towards the descending colon and sigmoid. The peritoneum is then retracted cranially to create a space between the anterior leaf of the broad ligament and the isthmic uterine area.

The surgeon continues the dissection towards the bladder, with the assistant cranially retracting the proximal peritoneum. Vesico-vaginal dissection is performed, ensuring hemostasis for small bleeders. The uterus is pushed towards the sacrum using a uterine manipulator with anterior and posterior valves. The peritoneum is opened in the right dissecting plane, cutting towards the right round ligament while controlling bleeding.

The surgeon approaches the right infundibulopelvic ligament, continuing lateral dissection near the ascending colon. The assistant retracts the bladder cranially, facilitating the cutting of the medial aspect of the left bladder pillar. This maneuver pulls the bladder down towards the anterior valve of the uterine manipulator, revealing the cervical vaginal vessel. Hemostasis is ensured throughout to maintain a dry operative field. The anterior dissection concludes with cutting the albin fascia and visualizing the anterior aspect of the vaginal valve.

The surgeon ensures hemostasis and controls the left infundibulopelvic ligament using the Clermont-Ferrand technique. The peritoneum surrounding the vessel is removed to apply sealing energy directly on the vessel. The ureter is visible, positioned far from the operative field. The posterior leaf of the broad ligament is opened toward the ismic uterine area, increasing the distance between the ureter and the infundibulopelvic ligament. The peritoneum is cut, and sealing energy is applied to the vessels, ensuring hemostasis over a two-centimeter area.

The surgeon applies sealing energy to the left uterine pedicle, ensuring hemostasis without cutting the vessel to prevent backflow. The peritoneum surrounding the left uterine artery is opened, taking care not to damage the uterine vein. The uterus is pulled to the opposite side, and the sealing energy is applied at the ismic area. The procedure is performed in real-time, focusing on controlling the uterine vein hemostasis.

The surgeon tracks the adnexa cranially while the assistant exposes the peritoneum. The peritoneum surrounding the infundibulopelvic ligament is cut, ensuring the ureter remains far from the operative field. The peritoneum is opened towards the posterior leaf of the broad ligament, following the Clermont-Ferrand technique. The surgeon cuts the peritoneum towards the upper part of the right uterosacral ligament, ensuring hemostasis. Sealing energy is applied to the infundibulopelvic ligament, maintaining a safe distance from the uterus. The pedicle is cut safely, and the connective tissue in the retroperitoneal space is opened.

The surgeon cuts the peritoneum along the posterior leaf of the broad ligament towards the upper part of the right uterosacral ligament, enhancing uterine mobility. The dissection continues near the posterior valve, and the right uterine pedicle is controlled by removing the peritoneum and applying sealing energy. The uterus is pulled to the left, increasing the distance between the uterine artery and the ureter. The vessel is sealed at the ismic area, and the pedicle is safely cut. The posterior vena is controlled, ensuring peritoneal hemostasis, and the dissection proceeds in the intrafascial plane towards the bladder, with hemostasis maintained.

The surgeon uses a monopolar needle to cut the vagina on the uterine manipulator, applying blend section current to incise the left lateral aspect and the upper part of the uterosacral ligament. Hemostasis is ensured, addressing a small bleeder with coagulating current. The right lateral aspect of the vagina is also cut, and the operative trocar valve is opened to remove smoke generated during the procedure.

The surgeon uses a monopolar hook to cut the posterior aspect of the vagina, ensuring hemostasis. The hook is placed on the left operative trocar to be perpendicular to the posterior aspect of the vagina, allowing for precise circumscription under visual control. The assistant aids in visualizing the section line. The uterus is extracted vaginally with the assistant tracking the uterine manipulator and uterus through the vagina. The uterus is removed transvaginally without delay for hemostasis, opting instead for direct suturing with Vicryl stitches. The vaginal area is rinsed to check for any significant bleeding.

The surgeon begins suturing from the lateral aspect of the vagina, threading the needle through the upper part of the uterosacral ligament to reduce postoperative vaginal prolapse. The rudder knot technique is used to close the vagina, with knots locked directly on the left aspect. The assistant rinses the pelvis to remove clots, and the uterine artery is visibly sealed. The needle is placed inside the vaginal mucosa and peritoneum, and the knot is closed with a rubber knot, ensuring both angles of the vagina are securely closed.

The surgeon closes the medial part of the vagina using a single stitch for the entire laparoscopic hysterectomy procedure. The rudder knot technique is employed for the initial knots, followed by intracorporeal knotting. The final stitch is an X knot, ensuring the vaginal mucosa is included within the stitch.

The assistants demonstrate the location of the vaginal mucosa, where the surgeon places the final stitch to reduce the risk of post-operative vaginal wall bleeding. The procedure concludes with the application of bipolar energy to ensure hemostasis, controlling small bleeders on the left part of the vagina and addressing minor bleeding from the upper part of the uterosacral ligament.
